questions.text: Growth in plants is unique because plants retain the capacity for unlimited growth throughout their life. This is due to the presence of:
questions.explanation: Meristems are tissues where cells have the capacity to divide and self-perpetuate, allowing for indeterminate growth.

questions.text: The form of growth where new cells are always being added to the plant body by the activity of the meristem is called:
questions.explanation: Plant growth is described as 'open' because new cells are continuously added by meristematic activity.

questions.text: In arithmetic growth, following mitotic cell division, only one daughter cell continues to divide while the other:
questions.explanation: In arithmetic growth, one cell remains meristematic while the other differentiates into a mature tissue cell.

questions.text: Which equation represents the geometric growth curve?
questions.explanation: Geometric growth is expressed as W1 = W0 ert, where r is the relative growth rate and e is the base of natural logarithms.

questions.text: The sigmoid curve is a characteristic of living organisms growing in a natural environment. The initial phase of slow growth is the:
questions.explanation: The lag phase is the initial period of slow growth before the exponential (log) phase starts.

questions.text: The ability of plants to follow different pathways in response to environment or phases of life to form different kinds of structures is called:
questions.explanation: Plasticity allows plants to develop different structures, such as different leaf shapes in aquatic vs terrestrial environments (heterophylly).

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of heterophylly due to the environment?
questions.explanation: Buttercup (Ranunculus) shows different leaf shapes in air and water, whereas cotton/coriander show heterophylly due to life phases.

questions.text: The process by which a differentiated cell regains the capacity of division under certain conditions is:
questions.explanation: Dedifferentiation allows mature cells (like parenchyma) to become meristematic again, as seen in the formation of interfascicular cambium.

questions.text: Auxins were first isolated from:
questions.explanation: Auxins were initially isolated from human urine, though they were first discovered by Darwin in canary grass coleoptiles.

questions.text: Which plant growth regulator is responsible for 'Bakane' (foolish seedling) disease in rice?
questions.explanation: Bakane disease is caused by the fungus Gibberella fujikuroi, which led to the discovery of Gibberellins.

questions.text: Which hormone is widely used to kill dicotyledonous weeds in lawns?
questions.explanation: 2,4-D (2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid) is a synthetic auxin used as a selective herbicide for dicot weeds.

questions.text: Gibberellins are used in the brewing industry to speed up the malting process. They are chemically:
questions.explanation: Gibberellins are terpenes, while auxins are indole compounds and cytokinins are adenine derivatives.

questions.text: Cytokinins were discovered as Kinetin by Skoog and Miller from:
questions.explanation: Kinetin (a modified adenine) was first crystallized from autoclaved herring sperm DNA; it does not occur naturally in plants.

questions.text: The hormone that helps in overcoming apical dominance is:
questions.explanation: Auxins promote apical dominance, while cytokinins promote lateral bud growth, thus counteracting the auxin effect.

questions.text: Which of the following is a gaseous plant growth regulator?
questions.explanation: Ethylene is the only gaseous hormone; it is synthesized in tissues undergoing senescence and ripening fruit.

questions.text: The horizontal growth of seedlings and swelling of the axis is a response to:
questions.explanation: Ethylene influences the 'triple response' in seedlings: slowing of primary root/stem elongation, thickening of the stem, and horizontal growth.

questions.text: Abscisic acid (ABA) is known as the stress hormone because it:
questions.explanation: ABA stimulates the closure of stomata and increases plant tolerance to environmental stresses like drought.

questions.text: The physiological response of a plant to the duration of light and dark periods is called:
questions.explanation: Photoperiodism refers to the plant's response to the relative lengths of day and night to trigger flowering.

questions.text: Plants that require the exposure to light for a period exceeding a well-defined critical duration are:
questions.explanation: Long day plants flower only when the day length is longer than a specific critical threshold.

questions.text: The phenomenon where flowering is either quantitatively or qualitatively dependent on exposure to low temperature is:
questions.explanation: Vernalisation prevents precocious reproductive development late in the growing season and ensures flowering occurs in spring after winter.

questions.text: Which hormone is used to induce parthenocarpy in tomatoes?
questions.explanation: Auxins like IAA and NAA are used to induce the formation of seedless fruits (parthenocarpy) in tomatoes.

questions.text: The site of perception of light/dark duration in plants is the:
questions.explanation: Leaves perceive the photoperiodic stimulus, which then migrates to the shoot apex to induce flowering.

questions.text: Bolting, i.e., internode elongation just prior to flowering in beet and cabbage, is promoted by:
questions.explanation: Gibberellins promote bolting in rosette plants like beet, cabbage, and many others.

questions.text: Ethephon is widely used in agriculture because it is:
questions.explanation: Ethephon is an aqueous solution that is easily absorbed and transported by the plant, slowly releasing ethylene.

questions.text: Which hormone acts as a general plant growth inhibitor and antagonist to Gibberellins?
questions.explanation: Abscisic acid (ABA) inhibits growth and seed germination, acting opposite to GA which promotes them.

questions.text: A growth rate that increases rapidly at first and then slows down as the organism reaches maturity is best described by:
questions.explanation: The sigmoid (S-shaped) curve is the standard growth curve for cells, tissues, and organs in a finite environment.

questions.text: One of the following is not a natural auxin:
questions.explanation: NAA (Naphthalene acetic acid) and 2,4-D are synthetic auxins; IAA and IBA are natural.

questions.text: The primary precursor of Ethylene is:
questions.explanation: Ethylene is synthesized from the amino acid methionine.

questions.text: The phenomenon of apical dominance is caused by:
options: High concentration of cytokinin in the apex
High concentration of auxin in the apex
High concentration of GA in the apex
questions.explanation: The shoot apex produces auxins which inhibit the growth of lateral (axillary) buds.

questions.text: Winter varieties of wheat are planted in:
questions.explanation: Winter wheat is planted in autumn, survives winter as seedlings, and is harvested in mid-summer.

questions.text: Zeatin is a natural cytokinin first isolated from:
questions.explanation: Zeatin was the first natural cytokinin discovered, isolated from corn kernels (Zea mays).

questions.text: Respiratory climacteric refers to:
options: Rise in respiration rate during fruit ripening
questions.explanation: Ethylene triggers a sharp increase in the rate of respiration in ripening fruits, called the respiratory climacteric.

questions.text: Which plant organ perceives the cold stimulus for vernalisation?
questions.explanation: Unlike photoperiodism (perceived by leaves), the stimulus for vernalisation is perceived by the shoot tip or embryo of a seed.

questions.text: The ratio of 'Efficiency Index' relates to:
questions.explanation: The relative growth rate (r) is often called the efficiency index, measuring the ability of the plant to produce new plant material.

questions.text: Which PGR was identified as 'Inhibitor-B', 'Abscission II' and 'Dormin' independently?
questions.explanation: These three substances were later proven to be chemically identical and were named Abscisic acid.

questions.text: The exponential phase of the sigmoid curve is also called:
questions.explanation: In the log (exponential) phase, growth is very rapid as the system uses available resources.

questions.text: Cytokinins help in delaying leaf senescence mainly by:
questions.explanation: Cytokinins delay senescence by promoting the mobilization of nutrients towards the treated parts.

questions.text: Most common auxin found in plants is:
questions.explanation: Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) is the most abundant and well-known natural auxin.

questions.text: Growth in plants is measurable. Which of the following is NOT typically used to measure it?
questions.explanation: Growth is measured by parameters like increase in fresh/dry weight, volume, area, length, or cell number.

questions.text: The critical factor in photoperiodism for many plants is the duration of:
questions.explanation: For many 'short-day' plants, it is actually the length of the uninterrupted dark period that is critical.
